backend.choice: For its control over the flow, strong typing, and ease of separating responsibilities.
backend.learning: Design clear contracts, isolate business rules, and treat errors as part of the product.

frontend.choice: Because it made it possible to build a clear interface without hiding the system's real behavior.
frontend.learning: Design predictable states, reusable components, and responsive experiences.

datos.choice: Because it fit the information model, queries, and performance needs of the project.
datos.learning: Model with integrity, traceability, and the cost of each query in mind.

herramientas.choice: Because it reduced friction in testing, collaboration, documentation, or deployment.
herramientas.learning: Automate repeatable tasks and maintain a verifiable delivery flow.

Dapper.choice: Because I needed explicit SQL queries, lightweight mapping, and control over performance.
Dapper.learning: Optimize queries without mixing data access with business rules.

Redis.choice: To support sessions and cache without increasing repeated database access.
Redis.learning: Design expiration, renewal, and fallbacks for distributed state.

SignalR.choice: To deliver real-time updates with a natural integration into .NET.
SignalR.learning: Model small events and keep the user experience synchronized.

ASP.NET Core.choice: For its modular architecture, integrated security, and ecosystem for maintainable applications.
ASP.NET Core.learning: Separate controllers, services, repositories, and middleware with clear responsibilities.

React.choice: To build interaction through reusable components and predictable state.
React.learning: Compose interfaces without coupling presentation to data.

TypeScript.choice: To catch errors before runtime and maintain clear contracts between components.
TypeScript.learning: Model data and interface states with useful, non-decorative types.

JWT.choice: To protect APIs with decoupled authentication and verifiable contracts.
JWT.learning: Separate identity, authorization, and credential lifetime.

decisions.question: Why Dapper?
decisions.answer: Because critical modules needed explicit, fast SQL queries that were easy to optimize.

decisions.question: Why Redis?
decisions.answer: To maintain resilient sessions and reduce repeated database access.

decisions.question: Why ASP.NET Core?
decisions.answer: For its modular architecture, strong typing, and ecosystem designed for maintainable applications.

decisions.question: Why SignalR?
decisions.answer: To communicate changes in real time without forcing the user to reload the interface.

decisions.question: Why JWT?
decisions.answer: To decouple authentication and protect API contracts predictably.

ccss.title: Institutional management

projects.title: Casa Net
projects.label: Web platform · Personal project
projects.description: A real estate ecosystem built with .NET 8: property listings, maps, image uploads, authentication, notifications, and a complete administrative experience.
projects.problem: Publishing, organizing, and browsing properties often involves scattered information, manual processes, and different experiences for clients and administrators.
projects.objective: Build a comprehensive real estate platform that centralized properties, clients, locations, files, and communication without sacrificing speed or clarity.
projects.participation: I designed and developed the end-to-end solution: architecture, data model, backend, Razor views, authentication, integrations, file uploads, and administrative experience.
projects.architecture: The application uses a layered MVC architecture. Controllers coordinate business services and Dapper repositories; MySQL persists information, Redis manages sessions and cache, SignalR distributes real-time events, and external services handle email, OAuth, maps, and images.
challenges: Maintaining fast, resilient sessions with Redis.
Processing images and files without affecting the publishing experience.
Synchronizing real-time notifications across administrative areas.
Integrating OAuth, reCAPTCHA, maps, and email into a coherent flow.
projects.result: A production-deployed platform with complete flows for real estate publishing, search, administration, and communication.
projects.learnings: I deepened my experience in layered design, flow observability, web security, query optimization, and UX decisions for products with multiple user types.

projects.title: CCSS Institutional Management
projects.label: Graduation project · Real case
projects.description: An institutional system that automates administrative processes, applies business rules, improves traceability, and facilitates operational follow-up.
projects.problem: Administrative processes distributed across manual controls made traceability, availability checks, and timely follow-up of each request difficult.
projects.objective: Digitize institutional processes across seven buildings by centralizing requests, statuses, validations, and reports into a consistent operational experience.
projects.participation: I collaborated on requirements discovery, administrative module design, REST integrations, business validations, documentation, and functional testing with process users.
projects.architecture: The solution concentrates the interface and operational logic in Oracle APEX, uses PL/SQL for rules and procedures, Oracle Database for persistence, and connects components through REST APIs.
challenges: Translating real operational rules into consistent validations.
Maintaining traceability among requests, owners, and statuses.
Designing clear screens for users with different technical levels.
Documenting and validating flows with functional evidence.
projects.result: An institutional system designed to reduce manual tasks, improve operational control, and facilitate administrative process tracking.
projects.learnings: I strengthened user communication, requirements analysis, business-rule design, and the importance of validating a solution in its real context.

projects.title: Interbank Transfers
projects.label: Mobile application + API
projects.description: A secure flow for registering transfers, validating funds and authorizations, checking currency, displaying transaction details, and emailing confirmations.
projects.problem: A transfer must validate multiple conditions before execution and communicate the result clearly without duplicating operations or hiding errors.
projects.objective: Create a simple mobile flow backed by an API that validates balance, status, authorization, and transaction data before registering and notifying.
projects.participation: I developed the Flutter form, confirmation and detail dialogs, and the C# endpoints for registration, currency lookup, and email delivery.
projects.architecture: Flutter captures and validates the user's intent; a C# Web API applies business rules, queries and persists in SQL, and SMTP sends the transaction confirmation.
challenges: Coordinating interface and server validations without duplicating critical logic.
Preventing invalid operations due to balance, status, or authorization.
Handling responses and errors clearly on mobile.
projects.result: A complete, verifiable flow from data capture through registration and email notification.
projects.learnings: I improved API contract design, asynchronous state management, and the separation between experience validations and critical server rules.
